pi_items.source: Task description specifies 'Nitric Acid, Red Fuming' but metadata name field is abbreviated
Verified on Sigma-Aldrich, CAMEO Chemicals (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/4044), NIST, and multiple chemical suppliers

CAMEO Chemicals and multiple safety data sheets confirm red fuming nitric acid is incompatible with cellulose and sawdust (combustible organic materials). Accelerates burning of combustible material and may cause charring and ignition.
Verified on multiple chemical supplier databases
Verified on NIST (webbook.nist.gov), Sigma-Aldrich, ChemicalBook, and CAMEO Chemicals (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/1292)

CAMEO Chemicals and safety data sheets confirm perchloric acid reacts violently with cellulose materials (wood, paper) and organic materials at room temperature, resulting in increased fire and explosion risk. Strong oxidizing acid incompatible with organic absorbents.
Verified on multiple chemical supplier databases
Verified on Sigma-Aldrich, Merck Millipore, TCI Chemicals, CAMEO Chemicals (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/257), and CAS Common Chemistry

CAMEO Chemicals and safety data sheets specify use of inert, non-combustible absorbent material for bromine spills. Organic materials are listed as incompatible. Cellulose is documented as incompatible with bromine.
Verified on multiple chemical supplier databases
Verified on Sigma-Aldrich, Spectrum Chemical, Merck Millipore, Fisher Scientific, CAMEO Chemicals (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/890), and CAS Common Chemistry

CAMEO Chemicals documents that hydrogen peroxide solutions (especially >30%) decompose violently with organic solvents and materials, producing oxygen and water vapor pressure that can cause explosive bursting. Organic absorbents pose decomposition and catalytic decomposition hazards.
Verified on CAMEO Chemicals (https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/12191) and multiple chemical suppliers
Verified on Spectrum Chemical, Sigma-Aldrich, and AccuStandard

Web search results show mineral oil is compatible with cellulose and sawdust absorbents. Research indicates cellulose-based materials and sawdust effectively absorb mineral oil and petroleum products. CAMEO Chemicals does not list cellulose or sawdust as incompatible absorbents for mineral oil. Mineral oil is a non-combustible liquid compatible with organic absorbents.

pi_items.verified_value: Keywords are relevant fire/combustion hazard indicators that would appear in CAMEO Chemicals hazard sections for red fuming nitric acid
pi_items.source: Analysis of safety hazard language for strong oxidizing acids; keywords align with documented hazards of red fuming nitric acid

pi_items.verified_value: Keywords are relevant hazard indicators documented in CAMEO Chemicals and SDS for perchloric acid incompatibilities
pi_items.source: Analysis of documented incompatibilities; perchloric acid explicitly documented as reacting violently with cellulose (wood, paper) and organic materials

pi_items.verified_value: Keywords are relevant hazard indicators for strong oxidizers; bromine requires inert (non-organic) absorbents
pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals and safety data sheets for bromine specify use of non-combustible materials and recommend against organic absorbents

pi_items.verified_value: Keywords are relevant hazard indicators; decomposition and catalytic decomposition are documented hazards with organic materials
pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals and safety data sheets document decomposition hazards and catalytic decomposition risks when hydrogen peroxide contacts organic materials

privileged_info_summary: The task uses CAMEO Chemicals (a real public NOAA database) as its data source. All five chemicals have correct CAS numbers: Nitric Acid, Red Fuming (7697-37-2), Perchloric Acid (7601-90-3), Bromine (7726-95-6), Hydrogen Peroxide (7722-84-1), and Mineral Oil (8012-95-1). The expected safety status for organic absorbents (cellulose and sawdust) is verified as accurate: the first four chemicals are UNSAFE for use with organic absorbents due to documented incompatibilities with combustible/organic materials or decomposition hazards, while Mineral Oil is SAFE and compatible with cellulose and sawdust absorbents. All keywords are relevant to the documented hazards in CAMEO Chemicals entries.
